column1: text
Transformer architectures use self-attention mechanisms to process sequential data in parallel, enabling significant speedups over recurrent models.
Fine-tuning a pre-trained language model on a domain-specific corpus often yields better performance than training from scratch.
Retrieval-augmented generation combines a dense retrieval system with a generative model to produce factually grounded responses.
Gradient descent optimization adjusts model weights iteratively to minimize the loss function over a training dataset.
Batch normalization stabilizes training by normalizing layer inputs across the mini-batch, reducing sensitivity to initialization.
Embedding models map high-dimensional text into dense vector spaces where semantic similarity corresponds to geometric proximity.
The MTEB benchmark evaluates embedding models across 56 tasks including classification, clustering, retrieval, and semantic similarity.
Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors and is commonly used to compare text embeddings for retrieval tasks.
Contrastive learning trains models to bring similar examples closer in embedding space while pushing dissimilar ones apart.
Tokenization splits raw text into subword units, allowing models to handle out-of-vocabulary words without a fixed vocabulary.
Attention scores are computed as the dot product of query and key vectors, scaled by the square root of the dimension.
Vector databases like FAISS and Qdrant enable efficient approximate nearest neighbor search over millions of embeddings.
Knowledge distillation transfers knowledge from a large teacher model to a smaller student model while preserving performance.
Cross-encoder models re-rank retrieved candidates by jointly encoding query and document, at higher computational cost than bi-encoders.
The perplexity metric evaluates language model quality by measuring how well the model predicts a held-out text sequence.
Sparse retrieval methods like BM25 use term frequency and inverse document frequency to rank documents against a query.
Dense retrieval encodes queries and documents into fixed-size vectors and retrieves by nearest neighbor search in embedding space.
Zero-shot prompting asks a language model to perform a task without any task-specific examples in the input.
Instruction tuning trains language models on diverse task instructions to improve generalization across unseen task formats.
The encoder of a transformer model produces contextual representations of input tokens used downstream for various NLP tasks.
Model quantization reduces precision of weights from 32-bit floats to 8-bit or 4-bit integers to decrease memory footprint.
Pre-training on large corpora allows models to learn general linguistic patterns before fine-tuning on specific downstream tasks.
Semantic search retrieves documents based on meaning rather than exact keyword overlap, using embedding similarity.
Neural networks consist of layers of weighted connections that transform inputs through nonlinear activation functions.
Regularization techniques like dropout and weight decay reduce overfitting by penalizing model complexity during training.
Hyperparameter tuning involves systematically searching for optimal learning rate, batch size, and architecture configurations.
Sentence transformers produce fixed-size sentence embeddings by pooling token-level representations from pre-trained models.
The ReLU activation function outputs zero for negative inputs and the identity for positive inputs, enabling sparse representations.
Multi-head attention allows transformers to jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces simultaneously.
Data augmentation artificially expands training data through transformations like paraphrasing, back-translation, or noise injection.
Layer normalization normalizes activations within a single training example rather than across the batch dimension.
Positional encodings inject sequence position information into transformer inputs since attention is order-invariant by default.
RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) aligns language models with human preferences through preference labeling.
Causal language models predict the next token given all preceding tokens and are used for text generation tasks.
The softmax function converts raw logit scores into a probability distribution over the vocabulary during decoding.
Beam search generates text by maintaining multiple candidate sequences and selecting the highest-probability completion.
Few-shot prompting provides a small number of input-output examples in the prompt to guide model behavior.
Embeddings from large models like text-embedding-3-large capture richer semantic nuance due to higher dimensionality.
Model serving infrastructure must handle low-latency inference requests while managing GPU memory and batching efficiently.
Evaluation metrics for retrieval include NDCG, MAP, Recall@K, and MRR, each capturing different aspects of ranking quality.
Transfer learning reuses parameters learned on one task as initialization for a related task, reducing required training data.
Parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA add small trainable adapters to frozen model layers to reduce compute.
Mixture of experts architectures route input tokens to specialized sub-networks, increasing model capacity without proportional compute.
Word embeddings like Word2Vec represent words as dense vectors trained to predict surrounding context words.
Semantic textual similarity measures how similar two sentences are in meaning, typically scored between 0 and 1.
The loss function quantifies the discrepancy between model predictions and ground truth labels during training.
Chunking splits long documents into overlapping segments before embedding to fit within model context length limits.
Re-ranking with a cross-encoder improves retrieval precision by scoring query-document pairs jointly after initial retrieval.
Model collapse occurs when a generative model produces outputs with reduced diversity, often due to overfitting on training data.
The BEIR benchmark evaluates zero-shot retrieval generalization across diverse domains including biomedical, scientific, and news text.
